div.h2: seq_file: Why Simple proc_read Is Broken vs. seq_file Iterator Pattern

g.text: Output truncated at 4096 bytes — silent data loss
Offset miscalculation: repeated or skipped lines
Output of any size delivered correctly
Concurrent readers each get complete snapshots
BEFORE: Raw proc_read (broken for large output)
1 Must manually track how many bytes already sent
2 Buffer size is ONE page (4096 bytes) — silent truncation beyond
4 offset arithmetic differs per kernel version
5 Almost every driver in Linux 2.4 had bugs here
read() returns 0 → cat sees EOF → exits
single_release frees struct seq_file and its buf
Offset tracking | AUTO | Manual in old API
Buffer size limit | NONE | 4096 byte page cap
Partial read safety | AUTO | Driver's problem
seek (lseek) support| AUTO | Broken in most drivers
start: returns SEQ_START_TOKEN once, then NULL
next: immediately returns NULL (one item only)
Result: zero boilerplate for statistics files
Use start/next/stop when output is a list
start(pos): return net_device at index pos
Scales to thousands of items, zero copying
danger: buffer boundary can split a line
handler(buf, start, off, count, eof, data)

text.tspan: /* CORRECT: seq_file handles ALL the complexity */

g.text: stores mydevice_proc_show as the show fn
next(m, v, pos) → returns NULL (no more items)
If cat reads 128 bytes at a time
seq_file tracks read_pos across multiple read() calls
kernel only re-invokes start/show if pos advanced past buffer
No re-invocation of your show fn for same data
Line boundaries preserved — never splits mid-word
Buffer grows automatically via kvmalloc if needed
seq_file* m passed to all callbacks
